items.q: What is the capacity of short-term memory according to Miller?
items.why: Students confuse capacity (items) with duration (about 18-30 seconds without rehearsal). Miller's magic number describes how many CHUNKS, not how long.

items.correct: Learning by observing the consequences others receive for behaviour.
items.misconception: Being directly rewarded for copying someone.
items.why: Students confuse vicarious with direct reinforcement. 'Vicarious' means experienced through someone else - you see them rewarded/punished and adjust your own behaviour accordingly.
Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.q: Which sleep stage features rapid eye movements and vivid dreams?
items.why: It's REM. “Stage 1 NREM” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Activation-synthesis theory of dreaming was proposed by:
items.why: It's Hobson and McCarley. “Freud” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many hours is the circadian rhythm cycle?
items.why: Exact answer: 24 hours. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
